entries.tutorialSubtitle: Layers of filo pastry, walnuts, and honey syrup
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Walnut baklava is layers of buttered filo pastry packed with a spiced walnut filling, baked until deeply golden, then drenched with a honey and orange syrup while still hot. The syrup soaks into the filo over several hours as it cools, turning it from crisp to sticky and translucent. It keeps for several days at room temperature.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Filo rolls coiled into nests with pistachio filling
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Bird's nest baklava (osmaliye) are individual filo rolls shaped into round nests, filled with chopped pistachios, baked, and soaked in orange blossom syrup. Each nest is formed by rolling a strip of buttered filo around a thin dowel, then sliding it off and coiling it into a round. They look more elaborate than they are.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Caramel and toasted almond toffee, very thin
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Almond brittle is a thin sheet of amber caramel set with toasted flaked almonds, poured onto a tray and left to harden. It snaps cleanly into irregular shards. The process is fast: the sugar cooks in under 10 minutes. The most dangerous moment is pouring the hot caramel, which should be done steadily and without hesitation.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Chewy, buttery caramels cut into squares
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Soft caramels are made from cream, butter, sugar, and golden syrup cooked together to around 120 degrees C, poured into a tin, and cut into squares when set. The result is a dense, slightly chewy sweet with a deep caramel flavour. They take about 30 minutes of active work and set overnight. Wrapped in baking paper, they make excellent gifts.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: American Southern drop biscuits, large and rough
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Cathead biscuits are large American Southern drop biscuits, shaped by dropping rough mounds of soft dough from a spoon rather than rolling and cutting. The name refers to their size: each biscuit is as big as a cat's head. The dough is wetter than a rolled biscuit, which makes them faster to make and gives a softer, more irregular crumb.

entries.tutorialExcerpt: Classic ring doughnuts are made from a yeasted, enriched dough, proved until puffy, then fried in hot oil and glazed with a thin sugar icing while still warm. entries.tutorialSubtitle: American cookie dough pressed into a tin and baked
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Cookie bars are chocolate chip cookie dough pressed into a rectangular tin and baked as a single slab, then cut into squares or bars once cooled. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Crisp, spiced ginger biscuits from Cornwall
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Cornish fairings are ginger biscuits that crack across the top as they bake, leaving a rough, cratered surface and a strongly spiced crumb. They take their name from the fairgrounds and markets of Cornwall where they were traditionally sold. The dough is quick and the flavour forward: ginger, mixed spice, and a back-note of treacle.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Dark, warmly spiced Scottish scones
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Treacle scones are a Scottish variety made with black treacle, which gives them a deep, slightly bitter sweetness and a dark crumb. Mixed spice and cinnamon are the usual flavouring. They bake in 12 minutes and are best eaten warm, split and spread with cold butter. Denser than a plain scone and more interesting.
